questions.stem: An extended real-valued function $f$ on a measurable set $E$ is (Lebesgue) measurable if, for every $a \in \mathbb{R}$, the set:
questions.explanation: Measurability of $f$ is defined by requiring each upper level set $\{f > a\}$ (a preimage of the ray $(a, \infty]$) to be a measurable set. Equivalent formulations use $\{f \geq a\}$, $\{f < a\}$, or $\{f \leq a\}$. Continuity and finiteness conditions are neither necessary nor part of the definition.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Which of these four conditions is NOT equivalent to measurability of $f$?
questions.explanation: The conditions on the rays $\{f > a\}$, $\{f \geq a\}$, $\{f < a\}$, $\{f \leq a\}$ are all equivalent and each defines measurability. However $\{f = a\}$ being measurable for all $a$ is strictly weaker and does not imply measurability, so it is the odd one out.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Every continuous function $f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is:
options: measurable, because preimages of open rays are open hence measurable.
questions.explanation: If $f$ is continuous then $\{f > a\} = f^{-1}((a, \infty))$ is open, and open sets are measurable, so $f$ is measurable. No boundedness or monotonicity is required; continuity already guarantees measurability.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: A simple function is by definition a measurable function that:
options: takes only finitely many distinct values.
questions.explanation: A simple function takes only finitely many values, say $c_1, \dots, c_n$, and can be written as $\sum_k c_k \chi_{E_k}$ with measurable sets $E_k = \{f = c_k\}$. It need not be continuous or differentiable, and it is automatically bounded since it has finitely many values.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: The simple approximation theorem states that every nonnegative measurable function $f$ is:
options: the pointwise limit of an increasing sequence of nonnegative simple functions.
the derivative of a simple function.
questions.explanation: The simple approximation theorem says a nonnegative measurable $f$ is the pointwise limit of an increasing sequence $0 \leq s_1 \leq s_2 \leq \cdots$ of nonnegative simple functions, $s_n \uparrow f$. This is the foundation for defining the Lebesgue integral of $f$ as a supremum over simple functions.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: If $f$ and $g$ are measurable functions, which of the following is NOT guaranteed to be measurable?
options: A function obtained by altering $f$ on a non-measurable set.
questions.explanation: Sums, products, and pointwise maxima of measurable functions are measurable, as these operations are built from countable operations on level sets. But redefining $f$ on a non-measurable set can destroy measurability, since the new level sets may fail to be measurable. Only the last operation is unsafe.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: If $(f_n)$ is a sequence of measurable functions, then $\sup_n f_n$ is measurable because:
options: the supremum of measurable functions is always continuous.
$\{\sup_n f_n > a\} = \bigcup_n \{f_n > a\}$, a countable union of measurable sets.
questions.explanation: The level set identity $\{\sup_n f_n > a\} = \bigcup_n \{f_n > a\}$ expresses the supremum's level set as a countable union of measurable sets, hence measurable. The same kind of argument (using intersections) handles $\inf$, $\limsup$, and $\liminf$, so all these limit operations preserve measurability.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: If $(f_n)$ are measurable and $f_n \to f$ pointwise, then $f$ is:
questions.explanation: A pointwise limit of measurable functions is measurable, because $f = \limsup_n f_n$ and limsup of measurable functions is measurable via countable operations on level sets. This stability under pointwise limits is a key advantage of the measurable class over the continuous class.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: The phrase 'a property holds almost everywhere (a.e.)' on $E$ means the property holds:
options: except on a subset of $E$ of measure zero.
questions.explanation: A property holds almost everywhere if the set of points where it fails has measure zero. For example $f = g$ a.e. means $\{f \neq g\}$ is a null set. This notion lets analysis ignore negligible exceptional sets.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Littlewood's three principles are informal statements that, roughly, every measurable set is nearly a finite union of intervals, every measurable function is nearly continuous, and:
options: every pointwise convergent sequence of measurable functions is nearly uniformly convergent.
every integral is a Riemann integral.
questions.explanation: Littlewood's third principle says pointwise convergence is 'nearly' uniform convergence, made precise by Egoroff's theorem. The first principle is approximation of measurable sets by finite unions of intervals, and the second (Lusin's theorem) says measurable functions are nearly continuous.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Egoroff's theorem states that if $f_n \to f$ pointwise a.e. on a set $E$ of finite measure, then for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there is a measurable $F \subseteq E$ with $m(E \setminus F) < \varepsilon$ such that:
questions.explanation: Egoroff's theorem upgrades almost-everywhere pointwise convergence to uniform convergence off a set of arbitrarily small measure: on the large set $F$, $f_n \to f$ uniformly. The finiteness of $m(E)$ is essential, and the conclusion is about uniform convergence, not continuity or openness.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Which hypothesis in Egoroff's theorem cannot simply be dropped?
options: That the underlying set $E$ has finite measure.
questions.explanation: Egoroff's theorem requires $m(E) < \infty$; on a set of infinite measure the conclusion can fail. For instance $f_n = \chi_{[n, n+1]}$ on $\mathbb{R}$ converges pointwise to $0$ but not uniformly off any set of finite-measure complement. The finite-measure hypothesis is therefore indispensable.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Lusin's theorem states that a measurable function $f$ on $[a,b]$ is, for every $\varepsilon > 0$:
options: continuous when restricted to a closed set $F \subseteq [a,b]$ with $m([a,b] \setminus F) < \varepsilon$.
questions.explanation: Lusin's theorem makes precise the principle that a measurable function is nearly continuous: there is a closed set $F$, missing only a set of measure less than $\varepsilon$, on which $f$ restricted is continuous. It does not claim $f$ is a polynomial or differentiable, only continuous on a large closed subset.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: The characteristic (indicator) function $\chi_E$ of a set $E$ is measurable if and only if:
questions.explanation: The level sets of $\chi_E$ are among $\varnothing$, $E$, $E^c$, and the whole space, so $\chi_E$ is measurable exactly when $E$ is a measurable set. Thus characteristic functions translate set measurability directly into function measurability.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: If $f$ is measurable and $g = f$ almost everywhere (with the underlying measure complete), then $g$ is:
questions.explanation: Because Lebesgue measure is complete, modifying a measurable function on a null set keeps it measurable: $\{g > a\}$ differs from $\{f > a\}$ only by a subset of the null set $\{f \neq g\}$, which is measurable. So $g$ is measurable, though it need not be continuous or constant.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: The Dirichlet function $\chi_{\mathbb{Q}}$ (equal to $1$ on rationals, $0$ elsewhere) is:
options: measurable, because $\mathbb{Q}$ is a measurable (indeed null) set.
questions.explanation: Since $\mathbb{Q}$ is a Borel (and null) set, its characteristic function $\chi_{\mathbb{Q}}$ is measurable. It is nowhere continuous and bounded, and being equal to $0$ almost everywhere it even has Lebesgue integral $0$, in contrast to its non-existent Riemann integral.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: For a measurable function $f$, the positive part $f^+ = \max(f, 0)$ and negative part $f^- = \max(-f, 0)$ satisfy:
options: $f = f^+ - f^-$ and $|f| = f^+ + f^-$, with both parts measurable.
questions.explanation: The standard decomposition is $f = f^+ - f^-$ with $|f| = f^+ + f^-$, and both $f^+$ and $f^-$ are nonnegative measurable functions since max of measurable functions is measurable. This splitting is how the Lebesgue integral of a general $f$ is defined from the nonnegative case.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Which statement about the relationship between continuous and measurable functions is correct?
options: Every continuous function is measurable, but not conversely.
questions.explanation: Continuity implies measurability, but the converse fails: $\chi_{\mathbb{Q}}$ is measurable yet nowhere continuous. So the measurable functions form a strictly larger class, which is exactly why Lebesgue's theory handles limits that the Riemann theory cannot.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: A simple function is written in canonical form as $\sum_{k=1}^{n} c_k \chi_{E_k}$. For this to be a valid representation of a simple function, the sets $E_k$ should be:
questions.explanation: Each $E_k$ must be measurable so that $\chi_{E_k}$ is a measurable function and the finite linear combination is measurable. In canonical form the $E_k = \{f = c_k\}$ are disjoint measurable sets with distinct values $c_k$. They need not be intervals or singletons.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Why does the simple approximation theorem use an INCREASING sequence $s_n \uparrow f$ of simple functions for nonnegative $f$?
options: It is not increasing; it is arbitrary.
So that the Monotone Convergence Theorem can later define $\int f = \lim_n \int s_n$.
questions.explanation: The increasing approximation $s_n \uparrow f$ dovetails with the Monotone Convergence Theorem, letting the Lebesgue integral be defined as $\int f = \lim_n \int s_n = \sup_n \int s_n$ in a well-defined, order-respecting way. Monotonicity guarantees the limit of integrals exists in $[0, \infty]$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Egoroff's theorem is the rigorous form of which of Littlewood's three principles?
options: Nearly every pointwise convergent sequence is uniformly convergent.
Nearly every measurable set is a finite union of intervals.
Nearly every measurable function is continuous.
questions.explanation: Egoroff's theorem precisely realises the third principle, that pointwise (a.e.) convergence is nearly uniform convergence, off a set of small measure. The first principle is set approximation, and the second, that measurable functions are nearly continuous, is Lusin's theorem.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Lusin's theorem is the rigorous form of which of Littlewood's three principles?
options: Every pointwise limit is nearly uniform.
Every measurable function is nearly continuous.
Every measurable set is nearly open.
Every function is nearly a polynomial.
questions.explanation: Lusin's theorem makes precise the second principle: a measurable function agrees with a continuous function off a set of arbitrarily small measure, so it is 'nearly continuous'. Egoroff handles the near-uniform-convergence principle, a different statement.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Suppose $f$ is measurable. Which set is then necessarily measurable for real numbers $a < b$?
questions.explanation: The set $\{a < f < b\} = \{f > a\} \cap \{f < b\}$ is an intersection of two measurable level sets, hence measurable. More generally preimages of all Borel sets under a measurable $f$ are measurable, so such 'slab' sets are always measurable.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: If $\varphi$ is continuous and $f$ is measurable, then the composition $\varphi \circ f$ is:
questions.explanation: When $\varphi$ is continuous, $\{\varphi \circ f > a\} = f^{-1}(\varphi^{-1}((a,\infty)))$, and $\varphi^{-1}((a,\infty))$ is open hence Borel, so its preimage under the measurable $f$ is measurable. Thus continuous-after-measurable is measurable (note the order matters; measurable-after-continuous can fail).
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: A monotone function $f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is measurable because:
options: each set $\{f > a\}$ is a ray or half-line, which is measurable.
questions.explanation: For a nondecreasing $f$, the set $\{f > a\}$ is an interval of the form $(c, \infty)$ or $[c, \infty)$ (a half-line), which is measurable. Hence monotone functions are measurable, even though they may have (at most countably many) jump discontinuities and so need not be continuous.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Which statement about 'almost everywhere' equality and integration is correct?
options: If $f = g$ a.e. then $f$ and $g$ have different Lebesgue integrals.
If $f = g$ a.e. then $\int f = \int g$ whenever the integrals exist.
Almost-everywhere equality requires $f = g$ at every point.
Null sets affect the value of the Lebesgue integral.
questions.explanation: Changing a function on a null set does not change its Lebesgue integral, so $f = g$ a.e. implies $\int f = \int g$. This insensitivity to null sets is a defining feature of the Lebesgue integral and underlies treating a.e.-equal functions as the same in $L^1$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Defining measurability via level sets. Fill in the blanks.
questions.solution: A function $f$ is measurable provided the set $\{x : f(x) > a\}$ is a {{1}} set for every real $a$. This is the preimage of a ray. An equivalent condition uses the set $\{x : f(x) \ge a\}$, and yet another uses $\{x : f(x) < a\}$: the number of such equivalent ray conditions commonly listed is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: Measurability requires each upper level set $\{f > a\}$ to be measurable. The four standard equivalent ray conditions are $\{f > a\}$, $\{f \ge a\}$, $\{f < a\}$, and $\{f \le a\}$, so the count is $4$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Simple functions and approximation.
questions.solution: A measurable function taking only finitely many distinct values is called a {{1}} function. The theorem stating that every nonnegative measurable function is the pointwise limit of an increasing sequence of such functions is the simple {{2}} theorem.
questions.explanation: A function with finitely many values is a simple function. The result that nonnegative measurable functions are increasing pointwise limits of simple functions is the simple approximation theorem.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Littlewood's three principles and their rigorous forms.
questions.solution: The principle that pointwise convergence is nearly uniform convergence is made precise by {{1}} theorem. The principle that a measurable function is nearly continuous is made precise by {{2}} theorem. Both theorems quantify the exceptional set using a set of small {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Egoroff's theorem turns a.e. pointwise convergence into uniform convergence off a small set, and Lusin's theorem makes a measurable function continuous off a small set. Both control the bad set by making its measure small.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Stability of measurability. Decide whether each construction preserves measurability (assume $f, g$ measurable).
questions.solution: The sum $f + g$ is measurable: answer {{1}}. The pointwise limit of a sequence of measurable functions is measurable: answer {{2}}. The characteristic function $\chi_E$ is measurable exactly when the set $E$ is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Sums and pointwise limits of measurable functions are measurable, the latter since a pointwise limit equals a limsup of measurable functions. The indicator $\chi_E$ is measurable iff $E$ is a measurable set.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Continuity, measurability, and a.e. equality.
questions.solution: Every continuous function is measurable: this statement is {{1}}. Every measurable function is continuous: this statement is {{2}}. If $f$ is measurable and $g = f$ almost everywhere, then $g$ is measurable: this statement is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Continuity implies measurability (true), but the Dirichlet function is measurable and nowhere continuous, so the converse is false. By completeness of Lebesgue measure, altering a measurable function on a null set keeps it measurable, so the third is true.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)
